116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Dependency-graph integrity (use `leandag`)** — the `\uses{}` edges must reflect the real mathematical dependencies. `leandag` computes the actual DAG, so use it instead of eyeballing:
130	  - **Broken edges** — `leandag build --json` reports `unknown_uses` (a `\uses{}` pointing at a label no declaration defines). Every one is a must-fix.
131	  - **Missing edges** — read each proof: if it relies on a lemma/definition that its `\uses{}` does not list, the edge is missing. These don't show up as "broken"; you find them by reading the math against the declared `\uses{}`. Missing edges make the loop dispatch provers out of order, so flag them as correctness findings.
132	  - **Isolated declarations** — `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated --type theorem`, and the `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint` counts in `leandag stats`) lists nodes with **no `\uses{}` out and nothing using them**. An isolated *blueprint* node is almost always a *symptom of a missing edge*, not dead weight — so for each one decide and record a disposition (see below). Isolated `lean_aux` nodes are uncovered Lean helpers, a separate "needs a blueprint entry" signal, not removal candidates.
133	- **Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)** — it is good practice for a chapter that relies on a Mathlib-provided result to state it as an explicit anchor block marked `\mathlibok` (with `\lean{}` naming the real Mathlib declaration), rather than a bare `\uses{}` to nothing. Treat such anchors as *valid and done* — never flag them as ∞ holes or `remove` candidates. But **audit each `\mathlibok` claim for faithfulness**, because a wrong one makes the loop skip a real gap: does Mathlib actually contain the named `\lean{}` declaration, and does the stated form match it? Verify against Lean (`archon-lean-lsp`) when in doubt. A `\mathlibok` on a statement Mathlib does not provide — or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's — is a hard fail (treat like a fabricated citation). Also: where a broken `\uses{}` points at what is really a Mathlib result, the right `wire-up` is "add a `\mathlibok` anchor for it," not "invent a project lemma."
134	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
135	- **Rendering integrity (run `archon blueprint-doctor`)** — the blueprint must *render*: as the compiled leanblueprint site and in the dashboard. The doctor lints this deterministically — run `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once and triage every `malformed_refs` finding into your report:
136	  - **`undefined-macro`** — a chapter uses `\foo` that no `macros/*.tex` or chapter-local `\providecommand` defines: it renders as raw TeX everywhere. Recommend the definition (usually obvious from context: `\Z` → `\mathbb{Z}`, `\fppf` → `\mathrm{fppf}`); when the intent is genuinely unclear (you cannot tell what the writer meant), flag it as a correctness finding for the chapter's writer instead of guessing.
137	  - **`math-delim`** — interleaved `$ … \( … \) … $` delimiters that shred formulas mid-sentence; per-line locations are in the finding. Must-fix, writer-directive material.
138	  - **`literal-ref` / `bare-label`** — placeholder "REF" tokens and raw label ids in prose; each needs a `\cref{}` or the human-readable number.
139	  Group these per chapter in your findings so the dispatcher can scope one writer per damaged chapter.
140	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
141	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
142	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
143	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
144	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
145	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
146	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
147	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
148	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
149	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
150	
151	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
152	
153	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
154	
155	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
156	
157	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
158	
159	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
160	
161	## Always read everything
162	
163	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
164	
165	## Unstarted-phase proposals
166	
167	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
168	
169	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. (That table lists only *remaining* phases — finished ones live in `## Completed` and need no proposal.) For each remaining phase row whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
170	
171	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
172	
173	The proposal answers:
174	
175	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
176	
177	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
178	
179	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
180	
181	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
182	
183	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
184	
185	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
186	
187	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
188	
189	```
190	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
191	
192	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
193	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
194	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
195	
196	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
197	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
198	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
199	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
200	...
201	
202	**`\uses` skeleton**:
203	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
204	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
205	
206	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
207	
208	**References for writer**:
209	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
210	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
211	
212	**Subphase choices exposed**:
213	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
214	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
215	```
216	
217	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
218	
219	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
220	
221	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
222	
223	## Directive Format
224	
225	```markdown
226	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
227	
228	## Slug
229	<slug>
230	
231	## Strategy snapshot
232	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
233	
234	## Routes
235	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
236	
237	## References
238	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
239	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
240	
241	## Focus areas (optional)
242	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
243	
244	## Known issues
245	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
246	```
247	
248	## What you do
249	
250	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
251	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
252	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
253	   - Read the entire chapter.
254	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
255	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
256	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
257	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
258	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
259	6. **Audit the dependency graph with `leandag`.** Run `leandag build --json` (broken/unknown `\uses{}`, `unmatched_lean`, isolated count) and `leandag show isolated` once for the whole blueprint. Then triage each isolated **blueprint** node into exactly one disposition and record it:
260	   - **wire-up** — it *should* connect to the graph but an edge is missing or mislabeled. Name the `\uses{}` that should be added (and on which side). This is the common case and a correctness finding — recommend a writer fix, do NOT recommend removal.
261	   - **remove** — it is genuinely orphaned scaffolding: nothing in the goal's proof closure needs it, it isn't the goal, and it isn't a `\mathlibok`/reference anchor. Only then is removal the right call. Name the chapter + label so the plan agent can authorize a writer to delete it.
262	   - **keep** — it is intentionally standalone (the goal theorem with nothing above it, a deliberately isolated `\mathlibok` reference, etc.). Say why and move on.
263	   You **flag and recommend**; you never edit. The plan agent turns a `wire-up`/`remove` disposition into a blueprint-writer directive.
264	7. **Run the rendering lint.** `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once for the whole blueprint; triage every `malformed_refs` finding (`undefined-macro`, `math-delim`, `literal-ref`, `bare-label`) per the "Rendering integrity" bullet above, grouped per chapter.
265	8. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
266	9. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
267	10. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
268	
269	You may also use:
270	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
271	- **`archon blueprint-doctor [--json]`** — the deterministic rendering/structure lint (orphan chapters, broken refs, literal-REF, interleaved math delimiters, bare labels, undefined macros, covers problems). Read-only; run it once per review and triage its `malformed_refs` (see "Rendering integrity" above).
272	- **`leandag`** — your read-only window into the real dependency DAG. Run it freely: it never touches project source or the blueprint. (`leandag build` refreshes the derived `.leandag/` cache, which is regenerated deterministically from the current tree — that is a tool artifact, not a project edit, so it does not violate your read-only rule.)
273	  - `leandag build --json` — the structured build report: `unknown_uses` (broken `\uses{}`), `unmatched_lean` (`\lean{}` pointing nowhere), `conflicts`, and `summary.isolated`.
274	  - `leandag stats` — counts including `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint`.
275	  - `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated [--type theorem] [--chapter <c>]`) — the isolated declarations, which you triage per node (below).
276	  - `leandag show gaps` — blueprint nodes missing a `\lean{}`.
277	
278	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
279	
280	## Report format
281	
282	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md`.
283	
284	**CRITICAL COST RULE**: Your report must be extremely concise to save LLM tokens. Use dense bullet points, abbreviations, and zero conversational filler. DO NOT write paragraphs. Omit any section that has no findings. The plan agent only needs the facts.

316	- **soon**: Minor citation issue in `Bar.tex`.

323	Your final assistant message:
324	
325	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> chapters audited, <M> findings, <K> unstarted-phase proposals`
326	- Top-level summary counts (incomplete parts, proofs lacking detail, unstarted phases, etc.)

329	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.

332	
333	- **Read every chapter, no scope shortcuts.**
334	- **Cross-reference every strategy phase against existing chapters.** An unstarted phase is not a clean bill of health — it is an opportunity to write the blueprint now and enable parallelism.
335	- **Proposals are actionable, not informational.** Each proposal block must be concrete enough to serve as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Vague "chapter needed for X" notes are not proposals.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Under Claude Code, skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Under Claude Code, rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — under Claude Code this is installed as the `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source), providing slash commands `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc. Under other harnesses the slash-command surface is absent; the underlying skill scripts are still on disk under `.claude/skills/` and can be run directly from the shell.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts — that path is the same whatever harness you run under. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) surface as `mcp__*` tools: under Claude Code they are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json`; under other harnesses the loop wires the same servers in at launch.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned agent reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**A dispatch is a BLOCKING Bash call — wait by staying inside it.** `archon-subagent.py` is synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and returns only once the child finishes and its report is on disk). A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; wait for that task's result before acting. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — it is non-blocking (returns immediately, "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` / `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. To run independent subagents **in parallel**, put all their dispatches in **one Bash call**, each backgrounded with `&`, ending with `wait` — e.g. `python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name A … & python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name B … & wait` — which blocks until all finish (the whole wave as one blocking dispatch). Serialize only subagents that write the **same file** (separate sequential calls). The rule never changes: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required.

74	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:

92	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
93	
94	Two semantic markers:
95	
96	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
97	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
98	
99	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
100	
101	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake build <module>` (it uses `lake build`, not `lake env lean <file>`, so dependencies are built and missing-import failures aren't spurious), and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents should NOT add or remove `\leanok` as routine bookkeeping — leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).
102	
103	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.

The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
110	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
111	
112	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
113	
114	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
115	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
116	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

1	\chapter{Effective descent: restriction of the glued sheaf to a chart}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GlueDescent}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The gluing primitive \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} produces, out of a glue datum
7	\(D\) of schemes (index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap
8	immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), transition isomorphisms
9	\(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), glued scheme \(X\) with chart
10	immersions \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\)), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
11	\(\mathcal{M}\) as the descent equalizer
12	\[
13	  \mathcal{M} \;=\; \operatorname{eq}(a, b)
14	    \;\hookrightarrow\; \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
15	\]
16	of two parallel maps into the overlap product. That construction makes
17	\(\mathcal{M}\) but does \emph{not} yet consume the cocycle conditions (C1)/(C2) on
18	the transition family \((g_{ij})\). This chapter discharges the remaining content of
19	descent: it shows that restricting the glued sheaf back to a chart recovers the
20	input sheaf there. Concretely, the canonical \emph{restriction morphism}
21	\(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{M}_i\) -- the adjoint transpose along the chart
22	immersion \(\iota_i\) of the \(i\)-th equalizer projection -- is an isomorphism, and
23	it is here that (C1)/(C2) are used. The engine is that pullback along an open
24	immersion is, up to natural isomorphism, the site-level restriction functor
25	(\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}); being a right adjoint it
26	preserves the descent equalizer and the pushforward product, so the chart
27	restriction of \(\mathcal{M}\) is computed factor by factor. The non-formal input is
28	the \emph{overlap base change} \(\beta_{ij}\), which identifies the restricted
29	pushforward factor \(\iota_i^{*}(\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) with a pushforward of a
30	restriction of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) to the overlap, using the cartesianness of the
31	chart--overlap square.
32	
33	\section{The descent equalizer and its overlap base change}
34	\label{sec:gd_equalizer}
35	
36	\begin{definition}
37	[The pushforward product carrying the descent equalizer]
38	  \label{def:gr_glue_equalizer}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueProd}
40	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue}
41	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum and \((\mathcal{M}_i)_{i \in J}\) a family of sheaves of
42	  modules with \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\). Write
43	  \[
44	    P \;=\; \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
45	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
46	    Q \;=\; \prod_{(i,j)} (f_{ij} \circ \iota_i)_{*}\,\bigl(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\bigr)
47	  \]
48	  for the \emph{pushforward product} and the \emph{overlap product} of sheaves of
49	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The two descent legs of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue},
50	  \[
51	    P \;\underset{b}{\overset{a}{\rightrightarrows}}\; Q,
52	  \]
53	  are: on the \((i,j)\)-component, leg \(a\) restricts the \(i\)-th chart factor to
54	  the overlap \(V_{ij}\) using the unit of the pullback--pushforward adjunction along
55	  \(f_{ij}\) and the pushforward-composition comparison
56	  \((\iota_i)_{*} \cong (f_{ij} \circ \iota_i)_{*} \circ f_{ij}^{*}\); leg \(b\)
57	  restricts the \(j\)-th chart factor, transports it across the inverse transition
58	  isomorphism \(g_{ij}^{-1}\), and reindexes the immersion via the glue condition
59	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji}) \circ \iota_j = f_{ij} \circ \iota_i\). The glued sheaf
60	  \(\mathcal{M} = \operatorname{eq}(a,b)\) is the equalizer of this parallel pair;
61	  \(P\) is the object it embeds into and \(Q\) the object detecting the overlap
62	  agreement. The product \(P\) is the carrier on which the \(i\)-th projection (and,
63	  through it, the restriction morphism) is defined.
64	\end{definition}

66	\begin{lemma}
67	[Overlap base change of the chart square (\(\beta_{ij}\))]
68	  \label{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}
69	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueOverlapBaseChangeIso}
70	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
71	  For chart indices \(i, j \in J\) there is a natural isomorphism of functors
72	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_{U_j}) \to \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_{U_i})\),
73	  \[
74	    \beta_{ij} \;:\;
75	      \iota_i^{*}\,(\iota_j)_{*}
76	        \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
77	      (f_{ij})_{*}\,(t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*},
78	  \]
79	  identifying, for any sheaf \(\mathcal{N}\) on the chart \(U_j\), the restriction to
80	  the chart \(U_i\) of the pushforward \((\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{N}\) with the
81	  pushforward along \(f_{ij}\) of the restriction of \(\mathcal{N}\) to the overlap
82	  \(V_{ij}\) along \(t_{ij} \circ f_{ji}\). Here the chart restriction \(\iota_i^{*}\)
83	  and the overlap restriction \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\) are realised as the
84	  site-level restriction functors, isomorphic to the geometric pullbacks by
85	  \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}.
86	\end{lemma}
87	\begin{proof}
88	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
89	  The isomorphism is the cartesianness of the chart--overlap square, made site-level.
90	  The square
91	  \[
92	    \begin{array}{ccc}
93	      V_{ij} & \xrightarrow{\ t_{ij} \circ f_{ji}\ } & U_j \\
94	      \ \downarrow{\scriptstyle f_{ij}} & & \downarrow{\scriptstyle \iota_j} \\
95	      U_i & \xrightarrow{\ \ \iota_i\ \ } & X
96	    \end{array}
97	  \]
98	  is cartesian, and its cartesianness is visible already on underlying opens: for an
99	  open \(V \subseteq U_i\),
100	  \[
101	    \iota_j^{-1}\bigl(\iota_i(V)\bigr)
102	      \;=\; (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})\bigl(f_{ij}^{-1}(V)\bigr),
103	  \]
104	  a point of \(U_j\) lands in the image of \(V\) under \(\iota_i\) precisely when it
105	  comes, via the glue relation, from a point of the overlap lying over \(V\). Hence
106	  the two composite opens functors
107	  \[
108	    (\iota_i)_{!} \circ \iota_j^{-1}
109	      \;=\;
110	    f_{ij}^{-1} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})_{!}
111	  \]
112	  agree on objects, and -- morphisms of the opens poset being proof-irrelevant -- as
113	  functors. Both \(\iota_i^{*}(\iota_j)_{*}\) and \((f_{ij})_{*}(t_{ij}\circ
114	  f_{ji})^{*}\) are site-level pushforwards along these two (equal) opens functors, so
115	  the comparison is assembled from the pushforward-composition isomorphisms together
116	  with the natural isomorphism transporting one opens functor to the other:
117	  \[
118	    \beta_{ij}
119	      \;=\;
120	    (\text{pushforwardComp})
121	      \;\circ\;
122	    (\text{pushforward of the opens-functor equality})
123	      \;\circ\;
124	    (\text{pushforwardCongr})
125	      \;\circ\;
126	    (\text{pushforwardComp})^{-1}.
127	  \]
128	  The one substantive coherence to check is that the comparison glued from the
129	  object-level opens equality is natural in the open \(V\): on the section level the
130	  two restriction maps induced by the equal composite functors coincide because the
131	  connecting morphisms are the unique arrows of the opens poset between the identified
132	  opens. This is the pointwise compatibility that completes the \(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\)
133	  factor.
134	\end{proof}

136	\section{The restriction morphism and effective descent}
137	\label{sec:gd_descent}
138	
139	\begin{lemma}
140	[The chart restriction morphism of the glued sheaf]
141	  \label{lem:glueRestrictionHom}
142	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionHom}
143	  \uses{def:gr_glue_equalizer, def:scheme_modules_glue}
144	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be the glued sheaf of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. The
145	  \(i\)-th \emph{projection} of \(\mathcal{M}\),
146	  \[
147	    \pi_i \;:\; \mathcal{M} \longrightarrow (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i,
148	  \]
149	  is the equalizer inclusion \(\mathcal{M} \hookrightarrow P\)
150	  (\cref{def:gr_glue_equalizer}) followed by the \(i\)-th product projection. Its
151	  adjoint transpose along the chart immersion \(\iota_i\), under the
152	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \(\iota_i^{*} \dashv (\iota_i)_{*}\), is the
153	  \emph{restriction morphism}
154	  \[
155	    \mathrm{r}_i \;:\; \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \longrightarrow \mathcal{M}_i .
156	  \]
157	  Equivalently, \(\mathrm{r}_i\) is the unique morphism whose pushforward, precomposed
158	  with the unit, recovers \(\pi_i\).
159	\end{lemma}
160	\begin{proof}
161	  \uses{def:gr_glue_equalizer, def:scheme_modules_glue}
162	  The projection \(\pi_i\) is well-defined by \cref{def:gr_glue_equalizer}: the glued
163	  sheaf is the equalizer \(\operatorname{eq}(a,b) \hookrightarrow P\), and composing
164	  the inclusion with the \(i\)-th projection of the product \(P = \prod_i
165	  (\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) gives a map to \((\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\). The
166	  restriction morphism is then its image under the natural bijection
167	  \[
168	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}\bigl(\mathcal{M}, (\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\bigr)
169	      \;\cong\;
170	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_{U_i}}\bigl(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{M}_i\bigr)
171	  \]
172	  supplied by the adjunction \(\iota_i^{*} \dashv (\iota_i)_{*}\). The stated
173	  equivalent description is the unit--counit form of this transpose.
174	\end{proof}
175	
176	\begin{theorem}
177	[Effective descent: the chart restriction morphism is an isomorphism]
178	  \label{thm:isIso_glueRestrictionHom}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_glueRestrictionHom}
180	  \uses{lem:glueRestrictionHom, lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso,
181	    lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib, lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
182	  Assume the transition family \((g_{ij})\) satisfies the cocycle conditions (C1) and
183	  (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. Then for every chart index \(i\) the
184	  restriction morphism \(\mathrm{r}_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{M}_i\)
185	  (\cref{lem:glueRestrictionHom}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of
186	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules.
187	\end{theorem}
188	\begin{proof}
189	  \uses{lem:glueRestrictionHom, lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso,
190	    lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib, lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
191	  The chart pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) is naturally isomorphic to the site-level
192	  restriction functor (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}), which
193	  is a right adjoint; consequently \(\iota_i^{*}\) \emph{preserves limits}. Applying
194	  it to the descent equalizer \(\mathcal{M} = \operatorname{eq}(a,b)\) exhibits
195	  \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) as the equalizer of the restricted legs
196	  \(\iota_i^{*}a, \iota_i^{*}b\), and applying it to the product
197	  \(P = \prod_j (\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) exhibits \(\iota_i^{*}P\) as the product
198	  \(\prod_j \iota_i^{*}(\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\). The overlap base change
199	  \(\beta_{ij}\) of \cref{lem:glueOverlapBaseChangeIso} then identifies the \(j\)-th
200	  factor \(\iota_i^{*}(\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j\) with
201	  \((f_{ij})_{*}\bigl((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\bigr)\). Under these
202	  identifications the restricted descent data becomes the data attached to the chart
203	  \(U_i\) by the cover \(\{f_{ij}\}\) of \(U_i\), and the \(i\)-th factor (where
204	  \(j = i\)) is, by (C1), \(\mathcal{M}_i\) itself.
205	
206	  We produce an inverse \(\mathrm{s}_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\).
207	  Because \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is the equalizer of the restricted legs, it
208	  suffices to give a morphism \(\mathcal{M}_i \to \iota_i^{*}P\) -- equivalently a
209	  compatible family of morphisms into the factors -- that equalizes
210	  \(\iota_i^{*}a\) and \(\iota_i^{*}b\). The \(j\)-th component is
211	  \[
212	    \mathcal{M}_i
213	      \xrightarrow{\ \eta_{f_{ij}}\ }
214	    (f_{ij})_{*}\,f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i
215	      \xrightarrow{\ (f_{ij})_{*} g_{ij}\ }
216	    (f_{ij})_{*}\,(t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j
217	      \xrightarrow{\ \beta_{ij}^{-1}\ }
218	    \iota_i^{*}(\iota_j)_{*}\mathcal{M}_j ,
219	  \]
220	  the unit of the pullback--pushforward adjunction along \(f_{ij}\), followed by the
221	  pushforward of the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\), followed by the inverse of the
222	  overlap base change. That this family equalizes the two restricted legs is exactly
223	  the triple-overlap multiplicativity (C2), transported through \(\beta\); and the
224	  composite \(\mathrm{r}_i \circ \mathrm{s}_i = \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{M}_i}\) reduces,
225	  via the \(j = i\) component, to the self-identity (C1) together with the counit
226	  triangle identity of the adjunction. The reverse composite
227	  \(\mathrm{s}_i \circ \mathrm{r}_i = \mathrm{id}\) holds because both sides are
228	  equalizer maps with the same components into \(P\); two such agree once their
229	  factors agree, which is the separatedness of the sheaf equalizer over the chart
230	  cover. The limit computation underlying these equalities is the sheaf
231	  Mayer--Vietoris pullback square (\cref{lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib}), and the
232	  final identification of sections is the uniqueness-over-a-cover principle
233	  (\cref{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}). Hence \(\mathrm{r}_i\) is an isomorphism.
234	\end{proof}

236	\begin{definition}
237	[The restriction isomorphism of the glued sheaf]
238	  \label{def:glueRestrictionIso}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueRestrictionIso}
240	  % NOTE: this is the 1:1 home for the Lean decl `glueRestrictionIso`
241	  % (AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GlueDescent.lean). The block
242	  % `def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso` in Picard_GrassmannianQuot.tex is a stale

245	  \uses{thm:isIso_glueRestrictionHom}

247	  transition family \((g_{ij})\) satisfying (C1)/(C2). For each chart index \(i\) the
248	  restriction morphism \(\mathrm{r}_i\) (\cref{lem:glueRestrictionHom}) is promoted to

250	  \[
251	    \rho_i \;:\; \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{M}_i,

256	  projection, and over each overlap \(V_{ij}\) the two restrictions \(\rho_i, \rho_j\)
257	  differ exactly by the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\).
258	\end{definition}

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	Stating the triple-overlap multiplicativity of the descent datum, however, first
46	requires a small piece of base-change machinery for the pullback functor on sheaves
47	of modules: the three transition isomorphisms attached to a triple of charts live a
48	priori on three different overlaps, and to compare their composite they must be
49	transported to a single common overlap. The next two blocks supply exactly this
50	transport -- the pseudofunctorial comparison of iterated pullbacks
51	(\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) and its packaging along the triple-overlap data of a
52	scheme glue datum (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) -- after which the
53	cocycle condition can be expressed cleanly.
54	
55	\begin{definition}[Comparison of iterated pullbacks of sheaves of modules]
56	  \label{def:modules_pullbackComp}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}
58	  \mathlibok
59	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
60	  Let \(a : X \to Y\) and \(b : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of schemes. For a sheaf of
61	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\), pulling back first along \(b\) and then
62	  along \(a\) agrees with pulling back along the composite \(b \circ a\): there is a
63	  natural isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
64	  \[
65	    a^{*}\bigl(b^{*}\mathcal{M}\bigr)
66	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
67	    (b \circ a)^{*}\mathcal{M},
68	  \]
69	  natural in \(\mathcal{M}\), expressing that \(\mathcal{M} \mapsto \mathcal{M}\)'s
70	  pullback is a pseudofunctor on schemes. These comparison isomorphisms satisfy the
71	  pentagon (associativity) coherence for a triple of composable morphisms. This is the
72	  pullback half of the pseudofunctoriality of sheaves of modules and is supplied by
73	  Mathlib.
74	\end{definition}
75	
76	\begin{lemma}\leanok
77	[Base-change transport of a transition isomorphism to a triple overlap]
78	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}
79	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackBaseChangeTransport}
80	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
81	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) with charts
82	  \(U_i\), overlaps \(V_{ij}\), overlap immersions \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow
83	  U_i\), transitions \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\), and triple-overlap
84	  data \(t'_{ijk}\) with its compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\). For an index
85	  triple \((i,j,k)\) write
86	  \[
87	    V_{ijk} \;=\; V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}
88	  \]
89	  for the common triple overlap, with the two projections
90	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) and \(p^{ik}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ik}\), and
91	  let \(t'_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{jki}\) be the glue datum's triple
92	  transition, satisfying
93	  \[
94	    t'_{ijk} \,\circ\, p^{jk}_{jki} \;=\; p^{ij}_{ijk} \,\circ\, t_{ij}
95	    \qquad (t_{\mathrm{fac}}).
96	  \]
97	  Given per-chart sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and a transition isomorphism
98	  \[
99	    g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
100	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
101	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
102	  \]
103	  on the overlap \(V_{ij}\) (as in \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}), the
104	  \emph{base-change transport} of \(g_{ij}\) to the triple overlap \(V_{ijk}\) is the
105	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{ijk}}\)-modules
106	  \[
107	    \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
108	      \;:\;
109	    (f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
110	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
111	    (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j
112	  \]
113	  obtained by pulling \(g_{ij}\) back along \(p^{ij}_{ijk}\) and reassociating the
114	  iterated pullbacks via \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. With the analogous transports
115	  \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) (the first taken along
116	  \(t'_{ijk}\), so that \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identifies its source and target sheaves
117	  with those of \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\)), all three
118	  transported isomorphisms become endomorphism-composable: \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\)
119	  and \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) land on a common sheaf, and their composite is
120	  comparable to \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) on \(V_{ijk}\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp, def:gr_the_glue_data}
125	  \leanok
126	  The pullback functor on sheaves of modules is a pseudofunctor: for composable
127	  scheme morphisms it carries the comparison isomorphisms of
128	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, natural in the sheaf and coherent for triple
129	  composites. Pulling the transition isomorphism \(g_{ij}\) back along the projection
130	  \(p^{ij}_{ijk} : V_{ijk} \to V_{ij}\) yields an isomorphism between
131	  \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*} f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and \((p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}(t_{ij} \circ
132	  f_{ji})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\); applying \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} to each side
133	  reassociates these into the single-pullback form
134	  \((f_{ij} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) and
135	  \((t_{ij} \circ f_{ji} \circ p^{ij}_{ijk})^{*}\mathcal{M}_j\), giving
136	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\). For the \((j,k)\)-transition the same construction is
137	  applied along the triple transition \(t'_{ijk}\) rather than a bare projection; the
138	  glue datum's compatibility identity \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\),
139	  \(t'_{ijk} \circ p^{jk}_{jki} = p^{ij}_{ijk} \circ t_{ij}\), guarantees that the
140	  source of \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\) coincides with the target of
141	  \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) (both are the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}_j\) along the same
142	  morphism \(V_{ijk} \to U_j\)), so the two are composable as endomorphism-style
143	  isomorphisms over \(V_{ijk}\). The reassociations introduced by
144	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} are coherent (they satisfy the pentagon identity), so
145	  the composite \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\) and the third
146	  transport \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are isomorphisms between the same pair of
147	  sheaves on \(V_{ijk}\), and may be compared directly. This is the module-level
148	  analogue of the scheme-level triangle \(t'_{ijk}\)/\(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) and is the
149	  shape in which the multiplicative cocycle condition is stated below.
150	\end{proof}
151	
152	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap endpoint bridges]
153	  \label{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
154	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_src,
155	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_mid,
156	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueData_bridge_tgt}
157	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
158	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) and fix
159	  indices \(i,j,k\) with triple overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\).
160	  The three transports of \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} have
161	  endpoints pinned down by three scheme-morphism equalities on \(V_{ijk}\), each a
162	  pure consequence of the glue datum's structural identities:
163	  \begin{itemize}
164	    \item (\emph{src}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ f_{ij} = \mathrm{snd} \circ f_{ik}\)
165	      -- the defining pullback condition of \(V_{ijk}\);
166	    \item (\emph{mid}) \(\mathrm{fst} \circ (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})
167	      = (t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ f_{jk}\) -- the pullback condition
168	      composed with the glue datum's \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) identity;
169	    \item (\emph{tgt}) \((t'_{ijk} \circ \mathrm{fst}) \circ (t_{jk} \circ f_{kj})
170	      = \mathrm{snd} \circ (t_{ik} \circ f_{ki})\) -- the cocycle core, from the
171	      \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) associativity, the reversed pullback condition, the
172	      \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\) collapse and the glue datum's cocycle identity.
173	  \end{itemize}
174	  These three equalities align the source, middle, and target sheaves of the three
175	  transports so that the cocycle equation (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}
176	  typechecks.
177	\end{lemma}
178	\begin{proof}  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
179	  Each equality is an identity of morphisms in the category of schemes, proved by
180	  rewriting with the glue datum's axioms: \emph{src} is literally
181	  \(\mathrm{pullback.condition}\); \emph{mid} adds the \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)
182	  factorisation \(D.t_{\mathrm{fac}}\,i\,j\,k\); \emph{tgt} chains
183	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at \((j,k,i)\), the reversed pullback
184	  condition, an auxiliary collapse combining \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)-associativity at
185	  \((k,i,j)\) with \(t_{\mathrm{inv}}\), and finally the cocycle identity
186	  \(D.\mathrm{cocycle}\,i\,j\,k\). No sheaf-of-modules data enters; these are
187	  bookkeeping equalities of the underlying scheme cover.
188	\end{proof}
189	
190	% HISTORICAL (abandoned route): an earlier draft built the glued sheaf as a hand-rolled
191	% presheaf of compatible families (decls gluePresheaf / gluePresheafModule / gluePresheafIsSheaf).
192	% That route is abandoned; the gluing is now the equalizer of pushforwards described in the
193	% Construction paragraph of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}, which subsumes it.
194	
195	\begin{definition}
196	
197	\leanok
198	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
199	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
200	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
201	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:modules_pullbackComp,
202	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
203	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
204	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
205	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
206	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
207	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
208	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
209	  charts. Suppose given:
210	  \begin{itemize}
211	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
212	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
213	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
214	      \[
215	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
216	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
217	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
218	      \]
219	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
220	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
221	  \end{itemize}
222	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
223	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
224	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
225	  \((g_{ij})\):
226	  \begin{enumerate}
227	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
228	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
229	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
230	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each common triple
231	      overlap \(V_{ijk} = V_{ij} \times_{U_i} V_{ik}\) the base-change transports
232	      (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three transition
233	      isomorphisms satisfy
234	      \[
235	        \widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i} \circ \widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}
236	          \;=\; \widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j},
237	      \]
238	      where \(\widehat{g}_{ij}^{\,k}\), \(\widehat{g}_{jk}^{\,i}\),
239	      \(\widehat{g}_{ik}^{\,j}\) are the transports of \(g_{ij}\), \(g_{jk}\),
240	      \(g_{ik}\) to \(V_{ijk}\) through the scheme-level triple transition
241	      \(t'_{ijk}\) and its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\), reassociated by the
242	      pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp} so that all three land on
243	      the same sheaf -- the multiplicative, \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle
244	      condition.
245	  \end{enumerate}
246	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
247	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing is well-defined. Out of this
248	  data the construction produces a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
249	  \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\).
250	
251	  \emph{Construction.} The category \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of sheaves of
252	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has all limits, and both pushforward along a morphism and
253	  these limits preserve the sheaf condition; the glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is therefore
254	  built directly as a limit -- an \emph{equalizer of pushforwards} -- rather than through a
255	  hand-built presheaf of compatible families. Concretely, form the two parallel maps of
256	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
257	  \[
258	    \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
259	      \;\;\underset{b}{\overset{a}{\rightrightarrows}}\;\;
260	    \prod_{i,j} (\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}\,\bigl(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\bigr),
261	  \]
262	  where, on the \((i,j)\)-component, leg \(a\) pushes the \(i\)-th factor forward along the
263	  overlap immersion \(f_{ij}\) using the unit of the pullback--pushforward adjunction
264	  together with the pushforward-composition comparison
265	  \((\iota_i)_{*} \cong (\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}\circ f_{ij}^{*}\) (the dual of
266	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), and leg \(b\) pushes the \(j\)-th factor forward and
267	  additionally transports it across the inverse transition isomorphism \((g_{ij})^{-1}\)
268	  and the glue datum's compatibility identity, so that both legs land on the common overlap
269	  sheaf \((\iota_i \circ f_{ij})_{*}(f_{ij}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i)\). The glued sheaf is the
270	  equalizer
271	  \[
272	    \mathcal{M} \;=\; \operatorname{eq}(a,b) \;\hookrightarrow\; \prod_{i} (\iota_i)_{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i,
273	  \]
274	  the sub-sheaf of tuples of pushed-forward chart sections whose two overlap restrictions
275	  agree -- precisely the compatible families, now realised as a categorical limit rather than
276	  a bespoke presheaf. The cocycle conditions (C1)/(C2) on the family \((g_{ij})\) are
277	  \emph{not} consumed in forming this equalizer object: the limit exists for any family of
278	  transition maps. They are instead what make the equalizer descend correctly, and are
279	  exactly the hypotheses pinned down downstream when the restriction isomorphisms
280	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso} are produced. This specialises effective descent
281	  of sheaves of modules to the concrete open cover \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\)
282	  supplied by a scheme glue datum, and is the form in which the Grassmannian's
283	  tautological bundle and quotient are built.
284	
285	  The glued sheaf comes with three further structures, each recorded as its own block
286	  downstream: the \emph{restriction isomorphisms} \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}
287	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps
288	  \cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}; the \emph{characterisation up to unique
289	  isomorphism} by the restriction property \cref{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}; and the
290	  \emph{descent of morphisms} -- a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)
291	  commuting with the transition isomorphisms glues to a unique \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to
292	  \mathcal{N}\) with \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ
293	  \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\) \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}.
294	\end{definition}
295	
296	\begin{definition}\leanok
297	[Restriction isomorphisms of the glued sheaf]
298	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}
299	  % NOTE: the Lean decl `glueRestrictionIso` is pinned canonically by
300	  % `def:glueRestrictionIso` in Picard_GlueDescent.tex (its 1:1 home after the
301	  % iter-067 file split). This block is the downstream consumer's cross-reference
302	  % node; it carries no \lean{} pin to avoid a duplicate.
303	  \uses{def:glueRestrictionIso, def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
304	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
305	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be the glued sheaf of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}. For each
306	  chart index \(i\) there is a \emph{restriction isomorphism} of sheaves of
307	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules
308	  \[
309	    \rho_i \;:\; \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{M}_i,
310	  \]
311	  compatible with the transition isomorphisms: over each overlap \(V_{ij}\) the two
312	  restrictions \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\), transported to the common overlap, differ
313	  exactly by \(g_{ij}\).
314	\end{definition}
315	\begin{proof}
316	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
317	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
318	    \leanok
319	  By the equalizer-of-pushforwards description \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue}, a section of
320	  \(\mathcal{M}\) over an open of the form \(\iota_i^{-1}V\), restricted to the chart
321	  \(U_i\), is recovered from and recovers its \(i\)-th component
322	  \(s_i \in \Gamma(\mathcal{M}_i, \cdot)\) -- the \(i\)-th factor of the product
323	  \(\prod_i (\iota_i)_{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) into which the equalizer embeds; the
324	  chart-restriction bridge \cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso} identifies the pullback
325	  \(\iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}\) with this restriction, and the \(i\)-th projection
326	  \((s_\bullet) \mapsto s_i\) is the isomorphism \(\rho_i\) (with inverse extending a chart
327	  section by the overlap relation).
328	  The overlap compatibility is the defining relation of the compatible families: over
329	  \(V_{ij}\) the components \(s_i, s_j\) are related by \(g_{ij}\), which is precisely the
330	  statement that \(\rho_i\) and \(\rho_j\) differ by \(g_{ij}\) after transport to
331	  \(V_{ij}\); the triple-overlap coherence needed for this to be consistent is (C2),
332	  whose transports are aligned by \cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport} and
333	  \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}.
334	\end{proof}

336	\begin{lemma}[Uniqueness of the glued sheaf up to canonical isomorphism]
337	  \label{lem:gr_modules_glue_unique}
338	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue_unique}
339	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
340	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
341	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
342	  Suppose \(\mathcal{M}'\) is another sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules equipped with
343	  isomorphisms \(\rho'_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}' \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
344	  compatible with the \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps. Then there is a unique isomorphism
345	  \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) intertwining the restriction
346	  isomorphisms, i.e.\ with \(\rho'_i \circ \iota_i^{*}(\cdot) = \rho_i\) for every \(i\).
347	\end{lemma}
348	\begin{proof}
349	  \uses{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
350	  A morphism of sheaves of modules on \(X\) is determined by, and may be assembled from,
351	  its restrictions to the members of the open cover \(\{\iota_i\}\), agreeing on the
352	  overlaps -- the locality and gluing of sections
353	  (\cref{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}) applied to the internal hom. The
354	  intertwining condition forces the restriction over \(U_i\) to be
355	  \((\rho'_i)^{-1} \circ \rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \to \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M}'\);
356	  these local isomorphisms agree on each overlap \(V_{ij}\) because both
357	  \((\rho_i)\) and \((\rho'_i)\) are compatible with the \emph{same} transition family
358	  \((g_{ij})\) (\cref{def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso}). By locality they glue to a
359	  unique global isomorphism \(\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}'\) with the
360	  required property; uniqueness is the separation half of the same gluing.
361	\end{proof}

363	\begin{definition}[Descent of morphisms along the cover]
364	  \label{def:gr_modules_glueHom}
365	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glueHom}
366	  % NOTE: forward declaration (planned work); the \lean{} target is not yet realised.
367	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_glue, def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso,
368	    lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
369	  Let \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) be two glued data
370	  over the same glue datum \(D\), with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\) and
371	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i, \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i\). Given a family
372	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\)

374	  the pullbacks of \(\phi_i\) and \(\phi_j\) over \(V_{ij}\)), there is a unique morphism
375	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) with

377	    \rho^{\mathcal{N}}_i \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi \;=\; \phi_i \circ \rho^{\mathcal{M}}_i

395	\subsection{Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary morphism}
396	\label{sec:grquot_pullback_free}
397	
398	The functor of points (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}) acts on morphisms by pullback, so
399	it needs the pullback of the trivial bundle to be again trivial --
400	\(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}\) -- for an
401	\emph{arbitrary} scheme morphism \(\varphi\). Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison applies
402	only when the underlying site functor \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is \emph{final}; the
403	following three blocks remove that restriction for every morphism, and the resulting
404	pullback machinery preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness.
405	
406	\begin{lemma}\leanok
407	[The opens-pullback functor is final]
408	  \label{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
409	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.opensMap_final}
410	  For any morphism of schemes \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the inverse-image functor on opens
411	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi : \mathrm{Opens}(T) \to \mathrm{Opens}(T')\),
412	  \(U \mapsto \varphi^{-1}U\), is \emph{final}.
413	\end{lemma}
414	
415	\begin{proof}
416	
417	\leanok
418	  Finality means that for every open \(V \subseteq T'\) the structured-arrow category of
419	  opens \(U \subseteq T\) with \(V \le \varphi^{-1}U\) is connected. That category is
420	  nonempty and has the terminal object \(U = \top\) -- one always has
421	  \(V \le \varphi^{-1}\top = T'\), and every object admits a unique arrow to \(\top\) --
422	  and a category with a terminal object is connected.
423	\end{proof}
424	
425	\begin{lemma}\leanok
426	[Pullback of a free sheaf is free]
427	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
428	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso}
429	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
430	  For any scheme morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), there is a canonical
431	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{T'}\)-modules
432	  \[
433	    \varphi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\bigr)
434	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
435	    \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,\oplus I}.
436	  \]
437	\end{lemma}
438	
439	\begin{proof}
440	  \uses{lem:gr_opensMap_final}
441	  \leanok
442	  Mathlib's pullback-of-free comparison produces this isomorphism whenever the site functor
443	  \(\mathrm{Opens.map}\,\varphi\) is final; that finality is \cref{lem:gr_opensMap_final},
444	  which holds for every morphism, so the comparison applies unconditionally.
445	\end{proof}
446	
447	\begin{lemma}
448	
449	\leanok
450	[Free-pullback comparison is natural in the base morphism]
451	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom}
452	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_eqToHom}
453	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
454	  Let \(\varphi, \psi : T' \to T\) be morphisms with \(\varphi = \psi\) and \(I\) an index
455	  type. The two free-pullback comparisons \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} along \(\varphi\) and
456	  \(\psi\) agree after transporting the differing pullback sources by the canonical
457	  \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\): the composite of that transport with
458	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\psi)\) equals \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi)\).
459	\end{lemma}
460	\begin{proof}
461	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
462	  \leanok
463	  Substituting the equality \(\varphi = \psi\) collapses the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) to the
464	  identity and the two comparisons coincide.
465	\end{proof}
466	
467	\begin{lemma}
468	
469	\leanok
470	[Iso-level free-pullback cancellation]
471	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso}
472	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_trans_symm_eqToIso}
473	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
474	  For equal base morphisms \(\varphi = \psi : T' \to T\) and index type \(I\), the composite
475	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi) \circ \mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\psi)^{-1}\) is the
476	  \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) transport between the (differing) pullback sources of the free sheaf
477	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,\oplus I}\). Stated and proved generically in the base morphisms, so its
478	  use never forces evaluation of a concrete immersion; this is the load-bearing replacement
479	  for the cast chain in the self-identity \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id}.
480	\end{lemma}
481	\begin{proof}
482	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
483	  \leanok
484	  Substituting \(\varphi = \psi\) turns the composite into
485	  \(\mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi) \circ \mathrm{pullbackFreeIso}(\varphi)^{-1} =
486	  \mathrm{id}\), which is the \(\mathrm{eqToIso}\) of the now-trivial source equality.
487	\end{proof}
488	
489	\begin{lemma}\leanok
490	[Pullback preserves rank-\(d\) local freeness]
491	  \label{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}
493	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
494	  Let \(\varphi : T' \to T\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
495	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules locally free of rank \(d\)
496	  (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}). Then the pullback \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is
497	  locally free of rank \(d\) on \(T'\).
498	\end{lemma}
499	
500	\begin{proof}
501	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:modules_pullbackComp}
502	  \leanok
503	  Choose an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(T\) trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\),
504	  \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\). The preimages
505	  \(\{\varphi^{-1}U_i\}\) cover \(T'\). On each member the factorisation
506	  \(\varphi \circ \iota_{\varphi^{-1}U_i} = (\varphi|_{U_i}) \circ \iota_{U_i}\) of the
507	  restricted morphism, together with the pseudofunctor comparison of iterated pullbacks
508	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}), identifies
509	  \((\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M})|_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}\) with
510	  \((\varphi|_{U_i})^{*}\mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}\); the latter is
511	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\varphi^{-1}U_i}^{\,d}\) by \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. Hence
512	  \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{M}\) is locally free of rank \(d\).
513	\end{proof}

515	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
516	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
517	
518	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
519	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
520	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
521	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
522	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
523	
524	\begin{definition}\leanok
525	[Global section of the unit module]
526	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
528	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
529	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
530	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
531	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
532	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
533	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
534	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
535	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
536	  global function.
537	\end{definition}
538	
539	\begin{definition}\leanok
540	[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
541	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
542	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
543	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
544	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
545	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
546	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
547	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
548	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
549	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
550	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
551	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
552	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
553	\end{definition}
554	
555	\begin{lemma}\leanok
556	[Scalar endomorphism of the unit is the identity]
557	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_one}
558	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_one}
559	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
560	  The scalar endomorphism attached to the unit function \(1 \in
561	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is the identity of the unit module:
562	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
563	\end{lemma}
564	
565	\begin{proof}
566	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
567	  Multiplication by \(1\) is the identity on every section, so under the
568	  identification \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\) the section \(1\)
569	  corresponds to \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\).
570	\end{proof}
571	
572	\begin{lemma}\leanok
573	[Scalar endomorphism of the zero function is zero]
574	  \label{lem:gr_scalarEnd_zero}
575	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd_zero}
