637	\emph{Infrastructure: matrix automorphisms of the free sheaf.} To realise the
638	\(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transitions one must promote an invertible \(d \times d\)
639	matrix of global functions to an \emph{automorphism} of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf
640	\(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\), exactly as the chart quotient \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}
641	realises the universal matrix. The next blocks assemble this matrix-to-morphism
642	functor and record its two structural identities -- that it carries matrix
643	multiplication to composition and the identity matrix to the identity -- which are
644	the algebraic heart of the bundle cocycle.
645	
646	\begin{definition}\leanok
647	[Matrix endomorphism of the free sheaf]
648	  \label{def:gr_matrixEnd}
649	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd}
650	  \uses{def:gr_scalarEnd}
651	  Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), and
652	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) a matrix of
653	  global functions. The \emph{matrix endomorphism} \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M)\) is the
654	  endomorphism of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) whose
655	  \((p,q)\)-component, under the presentation of \(\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}\) as the
656	  finite biproduct of \(d\) copies of the unit module \(\mathbf{1}\), is the scalar
657	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{p,q})\) (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}). It is
658	  assembled from these components by the universal property of the rank-\(d\)
659	  biproduct, exactly as the chart quotient \(u^I\) is assembled
660	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). This is the substitute for the (non-existent)
661	  Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) endomorphism of a free sheaf'' primitive.
662	\end{definition}
663	
664	\begin{lemma}\leanok
665	[Matrix endomorphism carries multiplication to composition]
666	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}
667	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_comp}
668	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
669	  For \(M, N \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{O}_S))\) the matrix
670	  endomorphisms compose according to the matrix product, with the order reversed by
671	  the contravariance of the component indexing:
672	  \[
673	    \mathrm{matrixEnd}(M) \,\circ\, \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N)
674	      \;=\; \mathrm{matrixEnd}(N \cdot M).
675	  \]
676	\end{lemma}
677	\begin{proof}
678	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp, lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}
679	  The composition of two biproduct-assembled morphisms is again biproduct-assembled,
680	  with \((i,q)\)-component the sum over the middle index \(p\) of the composites of
681	  components, \(\sum_p \mathrm{component}_{i,p} \circ \mathrm{component}_{p,q}\) (the
682	  categorical matrix product over the biproduct). Each composite of scalar
683	  endomorphisms is the scalar endomorphism of the product,
684	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a)\circ\mathrm{scalarEnd}(b) = \mathrm{scalarEnd}(ab)\)
685	  (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_comp}), and a finite sum of scalar endomorphisms is the
686	  scalar endomorphism of the sum (\cref{lem:gr_scalarEnd_add}); hence the \((i,q)\)-component
687	  of the composite is \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl(\sum_p M_{q,p} N_{p,i}\bigr) =
688	  \mathrm{scalarEnd}\bigl((N\cdot M)_{q,i}\bigr)\), which is precisely the
689	  \((i,q)\)-component of \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(N\cdot M)\). The reversal of the factor
690	  order is the contravariance of the column/component indexing.
691	\end{proof}

770	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
771	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
772	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
773	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
774	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
775	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
776	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
777	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
778	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
779	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
780	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$

782	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
783	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
784	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
785	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
786	  \[
787	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
788	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
789	  \]
790	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
791	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
792	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
793	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
794	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
795	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
796	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
797	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).

799	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
800	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of

802	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
803	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),

808	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct

850	\begin{lemma}\leanok
851	[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
852	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
853	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
854	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
855	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
856	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
857	\end{lemma}
858	
859	\begin{proof}
860	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_iFree}
861	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
862	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
863	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
864	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
865	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
866	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
867	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
868	  \[
869	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
870	  \]
871	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
872	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
873	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
874	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
875	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
876	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
877	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
878	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
879	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
880	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
881	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
882	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
883	\end{proof}

885	\section{The \(\mathrm{GL}_d\) bundle transition cocycle}
886	\label{sec:grquot_bundle_cocycle}
887	
888	The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) is
889	obtained by feeding the gluing primitive \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} the per-chart free
890	rank-\(d\) sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\), the bundle transitions
891	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the two module cocycle hypotheses (C1)/(C2) that primitive
892	requires. The matrix cocycle for the Cramer inverses \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) is already in hand --
893	it is the content of the scheme-level cocycle \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} and the minor identities
894	\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities} -- but the gluing primitive consumes its
895	\emph{module-level} incarnation: an honest isomorphism of sheaves of modules together with
896	its self-identity and triple-overlap multiplicativity. The three blocks of this section
897	package exactly that, decoupling the construction of \(\mathcal{U}\) from the remaining
898	matrix-to-module transport bookkeeping. This is the only net-new infrastructure the
899	universal quotient requires beyond the gluing primitive itself.

998	  data to \(V_{IJK}\)) the Cramer inverses of the localised minors satisfy the
999	  multiplicative cocycle identity

1010	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\) is \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), and its \(K\)-minor is

1600	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
1601	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1602	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
1603	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
1604	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
1605	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
1606	  \[
1607	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
1608	  \]
1609	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
1610	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). At the
1611	  module level this overlap compatibility -- the equalising condition consumed by the
1612	  section-level descent \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom} -- is exactly
1613	  \cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}, whose proof routes the matrix identity above
1614	  through the rectangular pullback/composition API
1615	  (\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}) and the
1616	  adjunction transpose \cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}.
1617	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
1618	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
1619	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
1620	  \[
1621	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1622	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
1623	  \]
1624	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
1625	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
1626	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
1627	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
1628	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
1629	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
1630	\end{definition}
1631	
1632	\section{The functor of points}
1633	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
1634	
1635	The Grassmannian functor sends a scheme \(T\) to the set of \emph{equivalence classes} of
1636	rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). The following block packages a single such
1637	quotient, the equivalence relation on them, and the pullback action through which the
1638	functor acts on morphisms; the two coherence blocks after it isolate the comparison
1639	identities that make that action functorial.
1640	
1641	\begin{definition}[Rank-\(d\) quotients and their pullback action]
1642	  \label{def:gr_rankQuotient}
1643	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient,
1644	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.Rel,
1645	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_refl,
1646	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_symm,
1647	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.RankQuotient.rel_trans,
1648	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqSetoid,
1649	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback,
1650	        AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rqPullback_rel}
1651	  \uses{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso,
1652	        def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1653	  A \emph{rank-\(d\) quotient of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} on a scheme \(T\) is a triple
1654	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-modules
1655	  \(\mathcal{F}\) that is locally free of rank \(d\) (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank})
1656	  together with an epimorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) out
1657	  of the trivial rank-\(r\) bundle. Two such quotients \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\)
1658	  and \(y = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) are \emph{equivalent}, written \(x \sim y\),
1659	  when there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with
1660	  \(q \circ f = q'\) (equivalently \(\ker q = \ker q'\)). This relation is reflexive
1661	  (\(f = \mathrm{id}\)), symmetric (\(f \mapsto f^{-1}\)), and transitive (composition of the
1662	  witnessing isomorphisms), hence an equivalence relation -- the setoid whose quotient is the
1663	  functor's value at \(T\).
1664	
1665	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) the \emph{pullback action} sends
1666	  \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) to
1667	  \[
1668	    \psi^{*}x \;=\;
1669	      \bigl\langle\, \psi^{*}\mathcal{F},\;
1670	        \mathcal{O}_{T'}^{\,r} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1671	        \psi^{*}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \psi^{*}q\ }
1672	        \psi^{*}\mathcal{F} \,\bigr\rangle,
1673	  \]
1674	  re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via the free-pullback isomorphism
1675	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}. The result is again a rank-\(d\) quotient: the composite is
1676	  an epimorphism because pullback, a left adjoint, preserves epimorphisms and the
1677	  re-presentation is an isomorphism, and \(\psi^{*}\mathcal{F}\) is locally free of rank \(d\)
1678	  by \cref{lem:gr_pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank}. The pullback action respects the equivalence
1679	  relation -- given \(f\) witnessing \(x \sim y\), the pulled-back isomorphism \(\psi^{*}f\)
1680	  witnesses \(\psi^{*}x \sim \psi^{*}y\), by functoriality of pullback -- so it descends to a
1681	  map of equivalence classes.
1682	\end{definition}
1683	
1684	The two blocks below record the comparison identities, between the free-pullback isomorphism
1685	\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} and Mathlib's pseudofunctor structure on pullback, that make
1686	the descended pullback action of \cref{def:gr_rankQuotient} functorial.

1688	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1689	[Identity coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1690	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1691	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1692	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1693	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\), the canonical comparison morphism of unit
1694	  modules \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) -- the map underlying the
1695	  free-pullback isomorphism \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso} at a one-element index -- coincides
1696	  with the component at \(\mathbf{1}\) of the pullback-identity isomorphism
1697	  \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1698	\end{lemma}
1699	
1700	\begin{proof}
1701	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1702	  \leanok
1703	  The comparison morphism is the mate of \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathbf 1}\) under the
1704	  pullback--pushforward adjunction: the image of the identity of \(\mathbf 1\) through the
1705	  adjunction's hom-equivalence on the unit. For the identity scheme morphism the pushforward
1706	  is the identity functor, so the adjunction unit is itself an identity; Mathlib's
1707	  pullback-identity isomorphism is exactly the conjugate (left-adjoint transpose) of the
1708	  pushforward-identity isomorphism. Tracing the identity through the hom-equivalence and the
1709	  conjugate-transpose identity for the pullback-identity isomorphism shows the comparison
1710	  equals the \(\mathbf 1\)-component of that isomorphism.
1711	\end{proof}
1712	
1713	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1714	[Identity coherence at the free sheaf]
1715	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}
1716	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_id}
1717	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1718	  For the identity morphism \(\mathrm{id}_T\) and an index set \(I\), the free-pullback
1719	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*}(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1) \cong \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) coincides
1720	  with the component at the free sheaf \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\) of the pullback-identity
1721	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{id}_T^{*} \cong \mathrm{id}\).
1722	\end{lemma}
1723	
1724	\begin{proof}
1725	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}
1726	  \leanok
1727	  Both sides are cocones on the coproduct \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\); by coproduct
1728	  extensionality it suffices to check equality after precomposing with each coprojection
1729	  \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element case
1730	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}.
1731	\end{proof}
1732	
1733	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1734	[Composite coherence of the free-pullback comparison]
1735	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1736	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1737	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1738	  For composable scheme morphisms \(a : T'' \to T'\) and \(b : T' \to T\), the comparison
1739	  morphism of unit modules \((b \circ a)^{*}\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) factors as
1740	  \(a^{*}\) of the comparison for \(b\), the comparison for \(a\), and the component at
1741	  \(\mathbf{1}\) of the iterated-pullback comparison \(a^{*}b^{*} \cong (b\circ a)^{*}\)
1742	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}).

1745	\begin{proof}
1746	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, def:modules_pullbackComp,
1747	    lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1748	    \leanok
1749	  This is the composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id}: the mate
1750	  construction is compatible with composition of adjunctions, so the comparison for
1751	  \(b \circ a\) is the pasting of the comparisons for \(a\) and \(b\) reassociated by the
1752	  pseudofunctor comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}. The compatibility of the mate
1753	  with the adjunction hom-equivalences is supplied by
1754	  \cref{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}, the reusable conjugate-naturality engine. The
1755	  pentagon coherence of the iterated-pullback comparisons makes the factorisation
1756	  well-defined.
1757	\end{proof}
1758	
1759	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1760	[Mate/conjugate compatibility of the adjunction hom-equivalence]
1761	  \label{lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1762	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app}
1763	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2\) be two adjunctions and \(\alpha\) a natural
1764	  transformation between their left adjoints. For a morphism \(f\) the hom-equivalence of
1765	  \(\mathrm{adj}_2\) applied to \(\alpha_c \circ f\) equals the hom-equivalence of
1766	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1\) applied to \(f\), post-composed with the component at \(d\) of the
1767	  conjugate (mate) of \(\alpha\). This is the general naturality of the conjugate
1768	  construction against the two hom-equivalences; it is the reusable engine behind
1769	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}.
1770	\end{lemma}
1771	
1772	\begin{proof}
1773	
1774	\leanok
1775	  Both sides unfold to the same pasting of unit/counit triangles for the two adjunctions;
1776	  the equality is the standard compatibility of the mate of \(\alpha\) with the
1777	  hom-set bijections.
1778	\end{proof}
1779	
1780	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1781	[Composite coherence at the free sheaf]
1782	  \label{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1783	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso_comp}
1784	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1785	  The composite analogue of \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_id}: for composable \(a, b\) and an
1786	  index set \(I\), the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b \circ a\) at \(\bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\)
1787	  factors through \(a^{*}\) of the free-pullback isomorphism for \(b\), the one for \(a\), and
1788	  the iterated-pullback comparison \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}.
1789	\end{lemma}
1790	
1791	\begin{proof}
1792	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1793	  \leanok

1795	  coprojection \(\mathbf 1 \to \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\), which reduces to the one-element
1796	  composite coherence \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} (with the base-ring sheaf
1797	  tracked through each pullback).

1800	Together \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id} and \cref{lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1801	upgrade to the corresponding free-sheaf coherences -- the free-pullback isomorphism at the
1802	identity equals the component of the pullback-identity isomorphism, and likewise for the
1803	composite -- by coproduct extensionality over the decomposition
1804	\(\mathcal{O}^{\,\oplus I} = \bigoplus_I \mathbf 1\). These coherences discharge both
1805	functoriality laws (identity and composition) of the Grassmannian
1806	functor \cref{def:grassmannian_functor}.

1808	\begin{definition}
1809	
1810	\leanok
1811	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
1812	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
1813	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
1814	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
1815	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1816	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1817	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1818	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
1819	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1820	  % "represents the
1821	  %  contravariant functor
1822	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1823	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1824	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1825	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1826	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
1827	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1828	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
1829	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
1830	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
1831	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
1832	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
1833	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
1834	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
1835	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1836	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families

1857	\section{The universal property}
1858	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
1859	
1860	The representability theorem \cref{thm:grassmannian_universal_property} has two halves:
1861	the forward map \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u\rangle\)
1862	and its inverse, which manufactures a morphism \(T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) out of a rank-\(d\)
1863	quotient \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\). The forward map needs the tautological
1864	quotient to actually be a quotient -- that \(u\) is an epimorphism
1865	(\cref{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}). The inverse is built Zariski-locally and glued: for
1866	each size-\(d\) subset \(I\) one isolates the open locus \(T_I \subseteq T\) where the
1867	\(I\)-indexed columns of \(q\) form a basis of \(\mathcal{F}\), classifies \(T_I \to U^I\)
1868	there, and glues over the cover \(\{T_I\}\). The blocks of this section supply the four
1869	ingredients of that inverse: the iso-locus of a sheaf morphism (\cref{def:isoLocus}) and
1870	the fact that a morphism becomes invertible after restriction to its iso-locus
1871	(\cref{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}); the chart locus \(T_I\) (\cref{def:chartLocus})
1872	and the proof that the chart loci cover \(T\) (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}); and the
1873	glued classifying morphism itself (\cref{def:grPointOfRankQuotient}).

1875	\begin{lemma}[The tautological quotient is an epimorphism]
1876	  \label{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}
1877	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient_epi}
1878	  \uses{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi, def:tautological_quotient}
1879	  The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
1880	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}) is an epimorphism of
1881	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-modules.
1882	\end{lemma}
1883	\begin{proof}
1884	  \uses{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi, def:tautological_quotient}
1885	  Being an epimorphism of sheaves of modules is local on the base: a morphism is epi
1886	  precisely when its restriction to every member of an open cover is epi, since the
1887	  cokernel is computed chartwise and a sheaf vanishing on a cover vanishes. The charts
1888	  \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and over \(U^I\) the tautological quotient,
1889	  transported through the trivialisation \(\mathcal{U}|_{U^I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
1890	  of \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}, is the chart quotient \(u^I :
1891	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}).
1892	  Each \(u^I\) is a split epimorphism: its \(I\)-minor is the identity \(1_{d\times d}\),
1893	  so the coordinate inclusion \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow
1894	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) on the \(I\)-columns is a section of \(u^I\), whence \(u^I\) is
1895	  epi (\cref{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}). As \(u^I\) is epi on every chart, \(u\) is epi.
1896	\end{proof}

1898	\begin{definition}[Iso-locus of a morphism of sheaves of modules]
1899	  \label{def:isoLocus}
1900	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isoLocus}
1901	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) a morphism of sheaves
1902	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The \emph{iso-locus} \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi) \subseteq X\)
1903	  is the open subscheme
1904	  \[
1905	    \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)
1906	      \;=\; \bigsqcup\,\bigl\{\, U \subseteq X \text{ open} \;:\;
1907	        \varphi|_U := (\iota_U^{*}\varphi) \text{ is an isomorphism} \,\bigr\},
1908	  \]
1909	  the supremum (union) of all opens \(U\) over which the pullback \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) of
1910	  \(\varphi\) to the open subscheme \(U\) is an isomorphism of sheaves of
1911	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules. Equivalently, a point \(t \in X\) lies in
1912	  \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) iff \(\varphi\) restricts to an isomorphism over some open
1913	  neighbourhood of \(t\). It is the largest open on which \(\varphi\) is invertible; Mathlib
1914	  has no iso-locus primitive for morphisms of sheaves of modules, so it is set up directly
1915	  as this supremum.

1918	\begin{lemma}[Stalk-wise iso criterion]

1926	  induced map on stalks \(f_t : \mathcal{F}_t \to \mathcal{G}_t\) is an isomorphism for every
1927	  point \(t \in X\), then \(f\) is an isomorphism.

1930	\begin{lemma}[A morphism is invertible over its iso-locus]
1931	  \label{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
1932	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
1933	  \uses{def:isoLocus, lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
1934	  For a morphism \(\varphi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of
1935	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the pullback of \(\varphi\) to the iso-locus
1936	  (\cref{def:isoLocus}),
1937	  \[
1938	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\varphi
1939	      \;:\;
1940	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\mathcal{M}
1941	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1942	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\mathcal{N},
1943	  \]

1949	  (\cref{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}) it suffices to show that the pulled-back
1950	  morphism induces an isomorphism on the stalk at every point \(t \in
1951	  \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\). By the definition of the iso-locus as a union of opens on
1952	  which \(\varphi\) restricts to an isomorphism (\cref{def:isoLocus}), each such \(t\) has an
1953	  open neighbourhood \(U \subseteq \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) with \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) an
1954	  isomorphism; restriction of an isomorphism is an isomorphism, and pullback commutes with
1955	  taking stalks, so the stalk of the morphism at \(t\) coincides with the stalk of
1956	  \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) at \(t\), which is invertible. Hence every stalk map is an
1957	  isomorphism, and the criterion concludes that the global pullback over
1958	  \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism.
1959	\end{proof}
1960	
1961	\begin{definition}[Chart locus of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
1962	  \label{def:chartLocus}
1963	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus}
1964	  \uses{def:isoLocus, def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_rankQuotient}
1965	  Let \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) be a rank-\(d\) quotient of
1966	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) on a scheme \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}) and \(I \subseteq
1967	  \{1,\dots,r\}\) a size-\(d\) subset. The \emph{chart composite} is the morphism of sheaves
1968	  of modules
1969	  \[
1970	    s_I \circ q
1971	      \;:\;
1972	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1973	      \overset{\ s_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1974	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1975	  \]
1976	  obtained by precomposing the quotient \(q\) with the inclusion \(s_I\) of the
1977	  \(I\)-indexed coordinates of the trivial bundle. The \emph{chart locus}
1978	  \(T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I) \subseteq T\) is the iso-locus
1979	  (\cref{def:isoLocus}) of this chart composite:
1980	  \[
1981	    T_I \;=\; \mathrm{isoLocus}(s_I \circ q),
1982	  \]
1983	  the largest open of \(T\) over which the \(I\)-columns of \(q\) form a basis of
1984	  \(\mathcal{F}\) -- i.e.\ over which \(s_I \circ q\) is an isomorphism. Over \(T_I\) the
1985	  quotient \(q\) is therefore presented, after trivialising \(\mathcal{F}\) by
1986	  \((s_I \circ q)^{-1}\), by a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-minor is the identity,
1987	  exactly the shape of the universal matrix \(X^I\) realising the chart quotient \(u^I\)
1988	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1989	\end{definition}
1990	
1991	\begin{lemma}[The chart loci cover \(T\)]
1992	  \label{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}
1993	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus_isOpenCover}
1994	  \uses{def:chartLocus, def:gr_rankQuotient, lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
1995	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1996	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
1997	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L823-L828).
1998	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1999	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
2000	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
2001	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
2002	  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
2003	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
2004	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
2005	  %  on $U^I_J$."
2006	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2007	  For a rank-\(d\) quotient \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)
2008	  on \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), the family of chart loci
2009	  \(\{\,T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I)\,\}_{\#(I) = d}\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}) is an open
2010	  cover of \(T\): \(\bigcup_{\#(I)=d} T_I = T\).
2011	\end{lemma}
2012	\begin{proof}
2013	  \uses{def:chartLocus, def:gr_rankQuotient}
2014	  Each \(T_I\) is open, being an iso-locus (\cref{def:isoLocus}). It remains to show the
2015	  \(T_I\) cover \(T\), i.e.\ that every point \(t \in T\) lies in some \(T_I\). Fix \(t\) and
2016	  work in an open neighbourhood \(V \ni t\) trivialising \(\mathcal{F}\),
2017	  \(\mathcal{F}|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V^{\,d}\) (such \(V\) exists because \(\mathcal{F}\) is
2018	  locally free of rank \(d\)); over \(V\) the quotient \(q\) is presented by a \(d \times r\)
2019	  matrix \(M\) of sections of \(\mathcal{O}_V\). Because \(q\) is an epimorphism
2020	  (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), its reduction modulo the maximal ideal of the local ring at
2021	  \(t\) -- the fibre map \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)
2022	  \cong \kappa(t)^{d}\) -- is surjective. A surjection of vector spaces \(\kappa(t)^{r}
2023	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa(t)^{d}\) carries some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard basis vectors to
2024	  a basis of \(\kappa(t)^{d}\); choosing such an index set \(I\) of size \(d\), the
2025	  corresponding \(d \times d\) minor \(M_I(t)\) is invertible over \(\kappa(t)\), i.e.\ its
2026	  determinant \(P_I = \det(M_I)\) does not vanish at \(t\). On the basic open
2027	  \(V_{P_I} = \{P_I \ne 0\} \subseteq V\), the section \(P_I\) is a unit, so the matrix
2028	  \(M_I\) is invertible and the chart composite \(s_I \circ q\) restricts to an isomorphism
2029	  there (this is the source mechanism: the locus where a minor determinant is invertible is
2030	  open, and the minor matrix admits an inverse on it). The implication ``minor invertible at
2031	  \(t\) \(\Rightarrow\) chart composite invertible on a neighbourhood'' is the Nakayama step:
2032	  invertibility of \((s_I \circ q)(t)\) on the fibre, together with finite generation, forces
2033	  invertibility on a neighbourhood. Hence \(t \in V_{P_I} \subseteq T_I\), and the \(T_I\)
2034	  cover \(T\).
2035	\end{proof}
2036	
2037	\begin{definition}[The morphism induced by a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2038	  \label{def:grPointOfRankQuotient}
2039	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.grPointOfRankQuotient}
2040	  \uses{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover, lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map,
2041	    def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle,
2042	    def:gr_glued_scheme}
2043	  Let \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) be a rank-\(d\) quotient of
2044	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) on a scheme \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}). The morphism
2045	  \[
2046	    \mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}(x) \;:\; T \;\longrightarrow\; \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
2047	  \]
2048	  is constructed as follows. Over each chart locus \(T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I)\)
2049	  (\cref{def:chartLocus}) the chart composite \(s_I \circ q\) is an isomorphism
2050	  (\cref{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}), so trivialising \(\mathcal{F}|_{T_I}\) by its
2051	  inverse presents \(q|_{T_I}\) as a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) of global functions on
2052	  \(T_I\) whose \(I\)-minor is the identity. Its \(d(r-d)\) complementary entries determine a
2053	  unique \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to \Gamma(T_I,
2054	  \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\), \(x^I_{p,q} \mapsto M^I_{p,q}\), hence by the \(\Gamma\)--\(\Spec\)
2055	  adjunction a unique \emph{chart morphism} \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
2056	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\), and thus
2057	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q|_{T_I}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}). The chart loci cover \(T\)
2058	  (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}), and on each overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two
2059	  presenting matrices are change-of-basis related by the invertible \(I\)-columns minor,
2060	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
2061	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}); hence the composites \(T_I \xrightarrow{\varphi_I} U^I
2062	  \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with the chart immersions agree on overlaps after the
2063	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\). By the cocycle condition (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) these
2064	  chart-local morphisms glue to a single morphism \(T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
2065	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}); this glued morphism is
2066	  \(\mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}(x)\), the local-to-global inverse of the universal
2067	  property: pulling the tautological quotient back along it recovers \(\langle \mathcal{F},
2068	  q\rangle\) up to equivalence.
2069	\end{definition}
2070	
2071	\begin{theorem}
2072	
2073	\leanok
2074	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
2075	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
2076	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
2077	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
2078	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
2079	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
2080	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
2081	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
2082	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
2083	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2084	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
2085	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
2086	  %  contravariant functor
2087	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
2088	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
2089	  %  set of all equivalence classes
2090	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
2091	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
2092	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
2093	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
2094	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
2095	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
2096	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
2097	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
2098	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
2099	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
2100	  \[
2101	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
2102	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2103	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
2104	    \qquad
2105	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
2106	      \;\longmapsto\;
2107	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
2108	  \]
2109	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
2110	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
2111	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
2112	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
2113	  universal family.
2114	\end{theorem}
2115	
2116	\begin{proof}
2117	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
2118	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
2119	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
2120	    def:grassmannian_functor, lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi,
2121	    def:grPointOfRankQuotient, lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover, def:chartLocus,
2122	    lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
2123	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
2124	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
2125	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
2126	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
2127	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
2128	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
2129	
2130	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
2131	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
2132	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
2133	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
2134	  the composite
2135	  \[
2136	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
2137	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2138	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
2139	  \]
2140	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
2141	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2142	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
2143	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
2144	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
2145	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
2146	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
2147	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
2148	
2149	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
2150	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
2151	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
2152	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
2153	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
2154	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
2155	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
2156	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
2157	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
2158	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
2159	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
2160	
2161	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
2162	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
2163	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
2164	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
2165	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
2166	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
2167	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
2168	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
2169	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
2170	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
2171	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
2172	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
2173	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
2174	
2175	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
2176	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
2177	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
2178	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
2179	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
2180	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
2181	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
2182	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
2183	
2184	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
2185	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
2186	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
2187	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
2188	\end{proof}

(`thm:grassmannian_universal_property`, second ingredient).

is a (split) epi (`chartQuotientMap_epi`). A family of morphisms out of the glued
sheaf is jointly reflected by the chart restrictions (the separation half of the sheaf
condition of the descent equalizer), so `q ≫ u = q ≫ v → u = v` follows once all chart
restrictions of `q` are epi. The joint-reflection lemma is the missing ingredient —
it shares its proof skeleton with `isIso_glueRestrictionHom` (mono-ness of
`E ⟶ ∏ (ι_I)_* M_I`). -/
theorem tautologicalQuotient_epi (d r : ℕ) : Epi (tautologicalQuotient d r) := by
  sorry

/-- **The tautological point of the Grassmannian**: the rank-`d` locally free quotient
`u : O^r ↠ U` on `Gr(d,r)` itself, packaged as a `RankQuotient`. Pulling it back along
`ψ : T ⟶ Gr(d,r)` realizes the forward direction of the universal property. -/
noncomputable def tautologicalRankQuotient (d r : ℕ) : RankQuotient r d (scheme d r) where
  F := universalQuotient d r
  q := tautologicalQuotient d r
  epi := tautologicalQuotient_epi d r
  locFree := universalQuotient_isLocallyFreeOfRank d r

/-! ### The Nitsure §1 inverse construction: chart loci, chart matrices, chart morphisms

For a rank-`d` quotient `x = ⟨F, q⟩` on `T` and a size-`d` subset `I ⊆ Fin r`, the
*chart composite* is `s_I ≫ q : O_T^d ⟶ F` (the `I`-indexed coordinate inclusion
followed by the quotient map) and the *chart locus* `T_I ⊆ T` is the largest open on
which it restricts to an isomorphism. The loci are open by construction (a supremum of
opens), cover `T` (Nakayama at each point), and over `T_I` the quotient is presented by
a `d × r` matrix of sections whose `I`-minor is the identity — its complementary
entries determine a ring map `R^I ⟶ Γ(T_I, O)` and hence a morphism `T_I ⟶ U^I` by the
Γ–Spec adjunction. These glue to the inverse `grPointOfRankQuotient` of the universal
property. -/

/-- The **iso-locus** of a morphism of sheaves of modules on a scheme: the supremum
(union) of all opens `U` such that the pullback of `φ` to the open subscheme `U` is an
isomorphism. This is the largest open on which `φ` is invertible. Project-local
(Mathlib has no iso-locus for morphisms of sheaves of modules). -/
def isoLocus {X : Scheme.{0}} {M N : X.Modules} (φ : M ⟶ N) : X.Opens :=
  sSup {U : X.Opens | IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.pullback U.ι).map φ)}

/-- Membership in the iso-locus: `t ∈ isoLocus φ` iff some open neighbourhood of `t`
pulls `φ` back to an isomorphism. Project-local. -/
lemma mem_isoLocus {X : Scheme.{0}} {M N : X.Modules} {φ : M ⟶ N} {t : X} :
    t ∈ isoLocus φ
      ↔ ∃ U : X.Opens, IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.pullback U.ι).map φ) ∧ t ∈ U := by
  simp only [isoLocus, TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_sSup, Set.mem_setOf_eq]

/-- Restriction stability of pullback-invertibility: if `φ` pulls back to an
isomorphism on `U` and `W ≤ U`, it pulls back to an isomorphism on `W` — transport
along the pseudofunctor comparison `pullback W.ι ≅ pullback U.ι ⋙ pullback (homOfLE)`.
Project-local. -/
lemma isIso_pullback_map_of_le {X : Scheme.{0}} {M N : X.Modules} (φ : M ⟶ N)
    {W U : X.Opens} (e : W ≤ U)
    (hU : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.pullback U.ι).map φ)) :
    IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.pullback W.ι).map φ) := by
  have h : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.pullback U.ι ⋙
      Scheme.Modules.pullback (X.homOfLE e)).map φ) := by
    change IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (X.homOfLE e)).map
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback U.ι).map φ))
    exact inferInstance
  exact (NatIso.isIso_map_iff
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (X.homOfLE e) U.ι ≪≫
      Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (X.homOfLE_ι e)) φ).mp h

noncomputable def chartLocus {T : Scheme.{0}} {r d : ℕ} (x : RankQuotient r d T)
    (I : Finset (Fin r)) (hI : I.card = d) : T.Opens :=
  isoLocus (chartComposite x I hI)

/-- **The chart loci cover `T`** (Nitsure §1, the Nakayama step): at each point `t`, the
fibre `F ⊗ κ(t)` is a `d`-dimensional quotient of `κ(t)^r`, so some `d` of the standard
basis vectors map to a basis; for that subset `I` the chart composite is an isomorphism
near `t` (a surjective endomorphism argument / Nakayama), i.e. `t ∈ T_I`.

PROOF ROUTE (scoped iter-067, not yet formalized): work in a trivialisation `V ∋ t`,
`F|_V ≅ O_V^d` (from `x.locFree`); the composite becomes a `d × d` matrix of sections of
`O_V`; epi-ness of `q` gives stalkwise surjectivity at `t`, so over the residue field
some `d`-column minor of the presenting `d × r` matrix has nonzero determinant; shrink
`V` to the basic open of that determinant, on which the matrix is invertible and hence
the composite an isomorphism (`matrixToFreeIso`). -/
theorem chartLocus_isOpenCover {T : Scheme.{0}} (d r : ℕ) (x : RankQuotient r d T) :
    TopologicalSpace.IsOpenCover (fun I : (theGlueData d r).J => chartLocus x I.1 I.2) := by
  sorry
